nativeRange.habitat: Muddy substrates near mangroves, deeper muddy channels, and continental shelf environments; occasionally associated with coral reef fringes and seagrass beds

diet.notes: Generally advised to feed frozen Mysis shrimp as the staple; vitamin-enriched adult brine shrimp are acceptable as a supplement but should not form the majority of the diet. Captive-bred specimens tend to adapt more readily to frozen foods. Amphipods and small crustaceans from live rock supplement the diet. Slow, deliberate feeders that benefit from two or more small target-feedings per day.

summary: A peaceful, mid-water Indonesian seahorse that tends to do best in a dedicated species tank with slow flow, plenty of hitching structures, and steady twice-daily feedings of frozen Mysis shrimp.
careNotes:
The Half-Spined Seahorse (Hippocampus semispinosus, described by Kuiter in 2001) is a relatively small seahorse native to the tropical West Pacific, with confirmed records from the East Alas Strait off Sumbawa in Indonesia and the Timor Sea along the northwest Australian shelf. In the wild it inhabits muddy substrates near mangroves, typically at depths of 10 to 80 metres.

The taxonomy of this species is unsettled. Some authorities treat H. semispinosus as a synonym of the Spiny Seahorse, H. spinosissimus (Weber, 1913), while others maintain it as a distinct valid species on the basis of the holotype specimen (NTM S10749-006, collected at Sumbawa), which shows a shorter trunk and smaller coronet than the larger H. spinosissimus. The name H. semispinosus continues to circulate widely in the aquarium trade and in Australian fisheries records.

Specimens vary naturally in colour: females tend toward dark red to yellow with grey saddle-like blotches, while males are generally darker — dark brown with creamy-white lateral patches. Like all seahorses, they use a prehensile tail to anchor themselves to branching gorgonians, macroalgae, seagrass, or artificial hitching posts, and they spend most of their time hitched rather than actively swimming.

Water flow should be kept gentle — spray bars or diffused outlets are generally advised over powerheads aimed directly into the aquarium, as strong currents exhaust seahorses quickly. Daily removal of uneaten food and detritus is recommended to maintain water quality. Temperature is generally advised not to exceed 26°C, as warmer water has been associated with increased susceptibility to bacterial infections (Vibrio spp.) in captive seahorses.

Captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught individuals, as they are typically already accustomed to frozen Mysis shrimp and tend to show better long-term survival in aquarium conditions. All seahorse species are listed under CITES Appendix II, which regulates their international trade; H. semispinosus is additionally protected under Australia's Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999.
breedingNotes: Like all seahorses, the Half-Spined Seahorse reproduces through paternal brooding. During courtship, the female transfers her eggs into the male's abdominal brood pouch — a specialised structure located beneath the tail — where the eggs develop and the male eventually gives live birth to fully formed juveniles. Captive breeding has been achieved with this species, though fry production has not been large enough to fully meet trade demand. Specific gestation periods for H. semispinosus are not well-documented in the aquarium literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: FishBase URL for H. semispinosus redirects to H. spinosissimus (Lourie et al. 2016 synonymy). Temperature range from H. spinosissimus data (22–27°C) used for cross-check; other parameters from aquarium sources.

additional.name: Fishes of Australia
additional.notes: Used for adult size (max 137 mm/13.7 cm per holotype description), native range (East Alas Strait, Timor Sea, NW Australian shelf), and taxonomic status. Treated as valid species here per Kuiter 2001 description.

additional.name: OceansGarden Aquaculture
additional.notes: Confirmed care level (easy/difficult split with LiveAquaria — see disagreement note), minimum tank size (30 gal), temperament (peaceful), reef safe (yes).

fish.breedingNotes: Males brood fry in an abdominal pouch. Captive breeding has been achieved, though production remains limited and detailed gestation data for this species specifically are not widely documented.
